questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Constitution of India:
I. - Article 25 protects religious faith and not
religious practices which may run counter to
public order, health or morality.
II. - Article 23 allows the State to impose
compulsory service for public purposes on
anyone with no obligation to pay for it.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 25** guarantees the freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate religion. However, this right is not absolute. It is explicitly subject to public order, morality, and health.
○ The Supreme Court (in the **Anand Marga case (1983)** regarding the Tandava dance in public with skulls) has ruled that **no religious practice is protected if it violates public order, health, or** **morality.** While religious faith/belief is internal and largely absolute, religious practices (external manifestation) are subject to State regulation if they harm societal order. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Article 23 prohibits traffic in human beings and "begar**" (forced labor without payment). However, the prohibition against forced labour is made subject to one exception. Under Article 23(2), the State can impose compulsory service for public purposes, and in imposing such service, the State shall not make any discrimination on grounds only of religion, race, caste or class or any of them. The State may thus exempt women from compulsory service for that will be discrimination on the ground of sex.
○ Therefore, the Government demanding services of teachers for census, election, and family planning duties is not violative of Article 23 (1). Such services fall within the meaning of “public purpose” under Article 23 (2) and these services can also be treated as national service which is a fundamental duty of every citizen under Article 51A. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
**Provision in the** **Constitution of India**
**Stated** **under**
I. - No law can be enacted if it is contrary to Fundamental Rights guaranteed under the Constitution
Part III
II. - Provision for early childhood care and education to children below the age of six years
Part IV
III. - Promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood amongst all the people of India
Preamble
IV. - Provide primary education in the mother tongue for children from linguistic minority groups
Part IV-A
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 13(2) of the Constitution** (Under Part III - **Fundamental Rights**) explicitly states that the State shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the Fundamental Rights, and any law made in contravention of this clause shall be void. Article 13 is the key provision as it gives teeth to the Fundamental Rights and provides for judicial review. Article 13 makes the judiciary, and especially the Supreme Court, as the guardian, protector and the interpreter of Fundamental Rights. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
● **Article 45 (Under Part IV - Directive Principles of State Policy)** mandates that the State shall endeavour to provide early childhood care and education for all children until they complete the age of six years. **So,** **pair II is correctly matched.**
● The duty "to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood amongst all the people of India" is provided under **Article 51A(e)**, which is a Fundamental Duty (Part IV-A of the constitution).
○ The **Preamble mentions fraternity,** which means a "**sense of brotherhood**" and emotional solidarity among all citizens. It serves as a binding force to ensure that the diverse people of India can coexist as a single nation. **So, pair III is not correctly matched**.
● The **instruction to provide education in the mother tongue** at the primary stage to children belonging to linguistic minority groups is stated under **Article 350A** (Part XVII - Official Language) and is not a Fundamental Duty.
○ **The Fundamental Duty related to education (Part IV-A, Article 51A(k))** refers to providing opportunities for education to children between 6 and 14 years. ○ **Article 350A** supplements **Article 29** as it directs every State, and every local authority within a State, to endeavour to provide adequate facilities for instruction in the mother tongue at the primary stage of education to the children of linguistic minorities. The Article also empowers the President to issue such directions to any State as he considers necessary or proper for securing the provision of these facilities. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to Indian polity, consider the
following statements:
I. - Every Minister and the Attorney-General
has a right to speak and take part in the
proceedings of either House of parliament,
but not in a joint sitting.
II. - A joint sitting cannot be held if Lok Sabha
is dissolved, even if it has been convened
already before the dissolution of Lok Sabha.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **concept of joint sitting (Article 108)** is taken from the Australian Constitution. The President can summon both the Houses to meet in a joint sitting for the purpose of deliberating and voting on the bill. It must be noted here that the provision of joint sitting is applicable to ordinary bills or financial bills only and not to money bills or Constitutional amendment bills.
○ **Attorney-General** is a constitutional post, a person who is qualified to be appointed a Supreme Court Judge can be appointed by the President as the **Attorney-General** **(Article 76(1)).** He/She holds office during the President’s pleasure and receives such remuneration as may be determined by him/her **[Article 76(4)]**. ○ **Article 88** of the Constitution grants **every Minister and the Attorney-General** of India the right to speak and take part in the proceedings of either House, **joint sitting of the Houses**, and any
committee of Parliament of which they may be named a member. However, the right to vote in a House accrues only to its members. ○ The rules of procedure with respect to joint sittings of the two Houses are to be made by the President after consulting the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and the Speaker of the Lok Sabha [Article 118(3)]. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **As per the Article 108(5)**, if the President has notified his intention to summon the Houses to meet in a joint sitting, and the Lok Sabha is dissolved after such notification, the joint sitting shall still be held. The dissolution of the Lok Sabha after the President has summoned the joint sitting does not annul the sitting. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
When the annual Union Budget is not passed by
the Lok Sabha, the Prime Minister submits the
resignation of the Council of Ministers.
**Statement II:**
The Council of Ministers is collectively
responsible to the Lok Sabha, and losing approval
of the Budget indicates that the government has
lost the confidence of the House.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India mandates that the "**Annual Financial Statement**" is laid before both Houses of Parliament. The Lok Sabha holds special powers regarding financial matters, including the passing of the Budget.
● The Union Budget process involves detailed discussions and voting on various demands for grants. Passing the Budget is **essentially a vote of confidence in the government's financial proposals.** If the Lok Sabha fails to pass the Union Budget, it implies that the government has lost the confidence of the majority of the Lok Sabha members.
● In the Indian parliamentary system, the **Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok** **Sabha**. This means they must maintain the support of the majority of the Lok Sabha members to remain in power. If the Lok Sabha rejects a critical financial proposal like the Union Budget, it is **considered a** **major defeat for the government and indicates a loss of confidence**.
● In such a scenario, the **Prime Minister and the entire Council of Ministers must resign**. This is because they no longer command the support necessary to govern. **So, statements I and II are correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: To protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures” is a provision made under the:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution (Forty-Second) Amendment Act, 1976**, added the Fundamental Duties of the Indian citizens in the Constitution, based on the recommendation of the **Swaran Singh committee.** For this purpose, a new **Part IVA** consisting of **Article 51A** has been added to the Constitution.
○ They are very useful tools to educate the public about the ethos of our constitutionalism.
● Under **article 51A(g)** it is the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures.
● Article 51A refers only to the Indian citizens unlike some Fundamental Rights, e.g., Articles 14 or 21,
which apply to all persons whether citizens or non-citizens.
● The Government of India set up **a committee (1999)** under the Chairpersonship of **Justice JS Verma,** **former Chief Justice of India**, “to work out a strategy as well as methodology of operationalising a countrywide programme for teaching Fundamental Duties in every educational institution as a measure of in-service training”. **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** PM Modi urges Indians to pay heed to duties to the nation on Constitution Day.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court:
I. 	 The President cannot seek advice on questions
regarding the validity of a statute already in force or a constitutional provision.
II. 	 The Supreme Court can refuse to tender its
opinion to the President in case of any preconstitutional treaty.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The concept of Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is borrowed from the Government of India Act 1935. Under **Article 143(1), the President can seek the opinion of the Supreme Court** on any question of law or fact of public importance which has arisen or is likely to arise. **This explicitly includes** **questions regarding the constitutional validity of an existing statute or even a proposed Bill**. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
○ An opinion should be delivered in open court [Article 145(4)] with the concurrence of the majority of Judges [Article 145(5)]. A Judge who does not concur has liberty to deliver a dissenting opinion [Article 145(4)]. The reference is to be heard by a Bench of not less than five Judges [Article 145(3)]. So far, the President has made sixteen references to the Supreme Court under its advisory jurisdiction.
● **Article 143(2)** deals with disputes arising out of pre-constitution treaties, agreements, covenants, engagement, sanad or other similar instrument, which is excluded from the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.
○ In matters of **Public Importance** (143(1)), the Supreme Court may refuse to give an opinion. ○ In matters of Pre-Constitution Treaties (143(2)), the Supreme Court must tender its opinion; it has no discretion to refuse.  **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** President has invoked the advisory jurisdiction to determine whether timelines can be set for the President and Governors to act on Bills sent by state Assemblies.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 A Bill pending for the assent of the Governor
will lapse if the State Assembly dissolves.
II. 	 If a Bill is sent to the State Legislature for
reconsideration and if it is passed again by the State Legislature, then the Governor should mandatorily give his assent.
III. 	When the Governor reserves a bill for the
consideration of the President, the Governor's role ceases for that Bill.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Articles **153 to 167 in Part VI of the Constitution deal with the state executive**. The state executive consists of the governor, the chief minister, the council of ministers and the advocate general of the state. The **governor is the chief executive (nominal executive head) head of the state**.
● **According to Article 196**, a bill pending in the State Legislature generally lapses on dissolution. But when a bill that has already been passed by the Legislative Assembly (and Council) and is pending assent from the Governor (or the President) does not lapse upon the dissolution of the Assembly. The Governor/President can still give assent to it, and it will become an Act. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● As per **article 200**, if the Governor returns a bill (other than a Money Bill) for reconsideration, and the **State Legislature passes the bill again** (with or without amendments), the **Governor "shall not** **withhold assent therefrom**". It becomes **mandatory** for the Governor to give assent. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **As per article 201,** when the Governor reserves a bill for the consideration of the President, the Governor's role ceases for that bill. The President may assent to the bill or withhold assent. If the President directs the Governor to return the bill for reconsideration and the House passes it again, it is sent back to the President.
○ Unlike the Governor (who must assent the second time), the President is not bound to give assent even if the state legislature passes it again. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court observed that no timeline can be prescribed or imposed by courts for the grant of assent by the Governor or the President.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to Indian polity, which of the
following statements are correct?
I. 	 Vice-President is elected by an Electoral
College, which consists of the members of
the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, including
nominated members.
II. 	 The Election Commission of India, in
consultation with the President, appoints
a Returning Officer for the election of Vice
President of India.
III. 	In India, the President is regarded as a
constituent part of the Parliament.
IV. 	 The Constitution provides that when vacancies
occur in the offices of both the President and
the Vice-President, the Chief Justice of India
will discharge the functions of the President.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Vice-President of India is the ex-officio chairman of the Rajya Sabha [Article 64]. According to Article 66(1), the Vice-President is elected by an electoral college consisting of the members of both Houses of Parliament.
○ Members of the Electoral College can vote according to their choice and are not bound by any party whip. This includes both elected and nominated members of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. This differs from the Presidential election, where nominated members are excluded. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● The **election to the office of the Vice-President** is governed by The Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Act, 1952 and The Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Rules, 1974.
○ **Under the Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Act, 1952 (section 3)**, the Election Commission of India (ECI) appoints the Returning Officer. The appointment is made in consultation with the Central Government (with the Ministry of Law and Justice), not the President. ○ By convention, the Secretary-General of the Lok Sabha and the Secretary-General of the Rajya Sabha are appointed as Returning Officers on a rotational basis. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Article 79 of the Constitution states that** there shall be a Parliament for the Union which shall consist of the President and two houses. Therefore, the President is an integral/constituent part of the Parliament, functioning to summon, prorogue, and give assent to bills, even though he/she is not a member of either house. **So, statement III is correct.**
● As per the Constitution the Vice-President acts as the President till the new President-elect enters upon her office [Article 65(1)]. The Vice-President also discharges the functions of the President when the president is unable to act owing to illness, absence or any other cause until the President can resume her duties [Article 65(2)].
○ The constitution (Article 70) only empowers the Parliament to make provisions for the discharge of the President's functions in contingencies not provided for in the Constitution. **It is the** **President (Discharge of Functions) Act, 1969**—a statutory law passed by Parliament that explicitly states the Chief Justice of India (or senior-most SC Judge) shall act as President when both offices are vacant. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** By convention, ECI appointed Garima Jain, Joint Secretary, Rajya Sabha Secretariat, and Vijay Kumar, Director, Rajya Sabha Secretariat, as Assistant Returning Officers to conduct election of Vice President.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Lok Adalats, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 Under the Legal Services Authority Act, 1987,
the State legislature has the requisite powers
to specify procedure for the determination of
any dispute coming before Lok Adalats.
II. 	 It is mandatory for the Centre and every State
Authority to establish permanent Lok Adalats
in respect of public utility services.
III. 	Lok Adalats have no jurisdiction over an
offence, which is not compoundable under
any law.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 39A mandates free legal aid for the poor to ensure justice for all and articles 14 and 22(1) obligate the State to ensure equality before the law and equal opportunity for justice. The Legal Services Authorities Act (1987) has established a nation-wide network to provide free and competent legal aid to the poor and to organise lok adalat for promoting equal justice. It has been given the status of a civil court. Its awards are enforceable, binding on the parties and final as no appeal lies before any court against them.
● **According to Section 22(2) of the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987**, the Lok Adalat itself has the requisite powers to specify its own procedure for the determination of any dispute coming before it. As per this every Lok Adalat shall have the requisite powers to specify its own procedure. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● The **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987** was amended in 2002, making it mandatory (**Section 22B (1))** for the Central Authority or, as the case may be, every State Authority to establish Permanent Lok Adalats in respect of public utility services (like transport, postal, power, etc.). **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Section 19(5) of the Act explicitly excludes non-compoundable offences from the jurisdiction of Lok** **Adalats.** Lok Adalats can only settle criminal cases if they are compoundable (i.e., where the law allows the parties to compromise, such as petty theft or traffic violations). Serious crimes (non-compoundable) cannot be settled here.
○ **A non-compoundable offense is a serious crime where the victim and the accused cannot** **settle the case through a compromise.** Unlike lesser offenses that can be withdrawn by the victim, non-compoundable offenses are considered to harm not only the individual but also society as a whole and must be tried fully in court. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**National Legal Services Authority (NALSA)**
● The National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) has been constituted under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 to provide free Legal Services to the weaker sections of the society and to organize Lok Adalats for amicable settlement of disputes.
● The Chief Justice of India is the Patron-in-Chief, NALSA and a Judge of the Supreme Court of India act as the Executive Chairman of NALSA.
● NALSA is housed at the Supreme Court of India.
● In every State, a **State Legal Services Authority** and in every High Court, a High Court Legal Services Committee have been constituted.
● The District Legal Services Authorities, Taluk Legal Services Committees have been constituted in the districts and most of the Taluks to give effect to the policies and directions of the NALSA and to provide free legal services to the people and conduct Lok Adalats in the State.
**Relevance:** Over 12.48 Lakh Cases Settled in One Day at Telangana Lok Adalat.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following organizations/bodies in
India:
I. 	 Central Bureau of Investigation
II. 	 National Commission for Safai Karamcharis
III. 	National Human Rights Commission
IV. 	 Rehabilitation Council of India
V. 	 National Security Council
VI. 	National Commission for Women
How many of the above are statutory bodies?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A statutory body is an organization or authority that has been established by a specific Act of Parliament or a State Legislature.
● **The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)** traces its origin to the Special Police Establishment which was set up in 1941 by the Government of India. **It was established by a resolution of the Ministry of** **Home Affairs (1963)**. It derives investigative powers from the Delhi Special Police Establishment Act, 1946, but the body itself is not created by the statute. It also helps the Central Vigilance Commission and
Lokpal. The **CBI functions under the administrative control of the Department of Personnel and** **Training (DoPT)** of the Ministry of Personnel. **So, point I is not correct.**
● The **National Commission for Safai Karamcharis (NCSK)** was constituted on 12th August 1994 as a statutory body by an Act of Parliament viz. ‘National Commission for Safai Karamcharis Act, 1993’, for a period of three years i.e. up to 31st March 1997. With the **lapsing** of the “the National Commission for Safai Karamcharis Act, 1993” with effect of 29.2.2004, the **Commission is acting as a non-statutory** **body** under the **Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment.** The Commission’s tenure is extended from time to time through Government Resolutions. **So, point II is not correct.**
● The **National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)** of India was established on 12 October 1993 under the **Protection of Human Rights Act (PHRA), 1993**. It is established in conformity with the **Paris** **Principles**, adopted at the first international workshop on National Institutions for the Promotion and Protection of Human Rights held in Paris in October 1991. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **Rehabilitation Council of India (RCI)** was set up as a registered society in 1986. In September 1992 the **RCI Act was enacted by Parliament,** and **it became a Statutory body on 22 June 1993.** The Act was amended by Parliament in 2000 to make it more broad-based. The mandate given to RCI is to regulate and monitor services given to persons with disabilities and to maintain a **Central Rehabilitation Register** of all qualified professionals and personnel working in the field of Rehabilitation and Special Education. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The **National Security Council (NSC)** of India is the apex body for national security, **established in** **1998 by an Executive Order, to advise the Prime Minister on security and strategic matters.** It operates under a three-tiered structure: the National Security Council itself, the Strategic Policy Group (SPG), and the National Security Advisory Board (NSAB). **The NSC is chaired by the Prime Minister**. **So, point V is not correct.**
● The **National Commission for Women** was set up as **a statutory body in January 1992 under the** **National Commission for Women Act, 1990** to review the Constitutional and legal safeguards for women; recommend remedial legislative measures, facilitate redressal of grievances and advise the Government on all policy matters affecting women. **So, point VI is correct.** **So, only one of the above is a statutory body.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Cabinet on February 7, 2025, approved a three-year extension of tenure for the National Commission for Safai Karamcharis (NCSK) upto 2028.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Neolithic period in India:
I. 	 The development of agriculture resulted
in surplus food production, which enabled the emergence of large, settled villages and permanent structures.
II. 	 The earliest archaeological evidence of
plant and animal domestication in the Indian subcontinent is associated with Neolithic sites located in southern India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Beginning around 10,000 BCE, this phase witnessed a fundamental shift from food gathering to food production, marked by the **domestication of plants and animals**. The adoption of agriculture **generated** **surplus food**, which in turn facilitated population growth, **permanent settlements, and the emergence** **of early village life**. This period also saw advances such as pottery-making and the construction of durable dwellings, reflecting a settled way of life. **So, statement I is correct.**
● In the Indian subcontinent, **the earliest evidence of Neolithic agriculture and animal domestication is** **associated with sites in north-western India,** many of which are located in present-day Pakistan. Important sites include Mehrgarh, Rana Ghundai, Sarai Kala and Jalilpur. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Among these, Mehrgarh provides the most extensive evidence of early Neolithic occupation dating back to about 7000 BCE, with clear indications of wheat and barley cultivation and the domestication of sheep, goats and cattle. This Neolithic cultural phase antedated and laid the foundations for the Indus Valley Civilisation. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
In the context of Indus Valley Civilisation,
consider the following pairs:
Site Major Finding
I. - Mohenjodaro Pashupati seal
II. - Lothal Walled citadel
III. Surkotada Pot burials
IV. Banawali Highly systematic drainage
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Mohenjodaro**, located in present-day Sindh (Pakistan) on the Indus River, was one of the largest Harappan cities and is notable for features such as the Great Bath, the Great Granary, and evidence of post-cremation burials. Archaeological finds include **seals depicting the Pashupati figure** and the Mother Goddess, the famous bronze statue of the Dancing Girl, a bronze buffalo, and the sculpted bearded man, reflecting advanced metallurgy and artistic skill. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Lothal**, situated in Gujarat at the confluence of the Bhogava and Sabarmati rivers, was an important maritime and trading centre, distinguished by its dockyard and port facilities, granaries, and evidence of rice husk. It also shows instances of double burial, where male and female were interred together. Unlike many Harappan sites, **Lothal’s citadel was elevated but not separately walled**, though the entire settlement was fortified, with internal divisions marked by walls, and the town was planned on a platform with distinct citadel and lower town areas. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Surkotada**, in Gujarat, is notable for its oval graves and **pot burial practices**, highlighting regional variations in Harappan funerary traditions. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Banawali**, located in Haryana on the Rangoi River, represents Pre-Harappan, Mature Harappan, and Late Harappan phases and is characterized by an oval-shaped settlement, findings of barley grains, lapis lazuli, and fire altars, along with radial street patterns and a **lack of systematic drainage**. **So, pair IV is not** **correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText: In the early vedic period, Vrajapati was:

options.a:
Chief of a pastoral clan responsible for ritual
and social leadership
options.b:
Permanent commander of the tribal standing
army
options.c: Officer who was in charge of pasture lands
options.d: Administrative head of a settled village unit

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The polity of the Rig Vedic period was that of a tribal society.** The chief of the tribe was the main political head and he was called rajan. The kings lived in multipillared palaces. They offered gifts of cattle, chariots and horse ornaments and gold to the priests. Rajan was a hereditary chief.
● The purohita (priest), **senani** (commander), **vrajapati (in charge of pasture lands)**, **spasa** (spy), **jivagribha** (police official), **madhyamasi** (mediator in disputes), **gramani** (head of the village), **dampati** or leulapa (head of family), and others were the main officials. In the Rig Vedic period, there was no official connected with the collection of taxes and hence no regular taxation was possible. Only voluntary offerings, viz. bali, from the people and also spoils of war formed the occasional income of the chief. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Slogan Meaning Source
I. - Satyameva Jayate
Truth alone triumphs
Mundaka Upanishad
II. - Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam
Assimilation of all societies into single culture
Maha Upanishad
III. Ahimsa
Paramo Dharma
Nonviolence is the highest duty
Chandogya Upanishad
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:** Ancient Indian philosophical texts such as the Upanishads and epics have contributed enduring ethical and metaphysical ideas. Many famous quotations are often attributed to these texts, but some associations are incorrectly assumed. A careful understanding of original textual sources is essential.
● **“Satyameva Jayate,”** meaning Truth alone triumphs, is found in the Mundaka Upanishad. The statement highlights the **moral and spiritual supremacy of truth in human life**. It stresses that adherence to truth ultimately leads to victory over falsehood. This phrase later became the national motto of India, reflecting its enduring philosophical importance. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **“Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam,”** which means the **whole world is one family,** occurs in the **Maha** **Upanishad**. The statement conveys a universal and inclusive worldview, transcending boundaries of nation, race, or religion. It emphasizes harmony, compassion, and collective coexistence. This idea is foundational to Indian philosophical universalism. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **“Ahimsa Paramo Dharma,”** meaning **non-violence is the highest duty**, is more prominently associated with the **Mahabharata** and later ethical and Dharmashastra traditions. While the concept of non-violence exists in Upanishadic thought, this exact formulation does not originate from Chandogya Upanishad. Hence, this pair is incorrectly matched. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, in only one of the above rows the given information is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Stone Age, Chalcolithic Age, Iron Age
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following metals in the correct
chronological order of their beginning of use in
ancient India, from the earliest to the latest:
I. 	 Iron
II. 	 Copper
III. 	Bronze
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Copper was the first metal discovered and used by humans in ancient India**, marking the **transition** **from the Stone Age to the Metal Age (Chalcolithic Period, or Copper Age).** This period in the Indian subcontinent is generally dated to around 3000 BCE to 500 BCE, and people used both stone and copper tools during this time.
● **People later learned to alloy copper with tin** (or sometimes arsenic) **to create bronze,** a harder and more durable metal. The use of bronze became a defining **characteristic of the highly urbanized Indus** **Valley Civilization** (Mature Period 2600–1900 BCE), which is also known as the **Bronze Age** **civilization**.
● **The Iron Age in India succeeded the Bronze Age**, with the use of iron becoming widespread around 1000 BCE (though some evidence suggests beginnings as early as 1800 BCE in certain regions). **Iron** **tools and weapons were more common in the later Vedic and subsequent periods.**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Ajivika Sect:
I. 	 It was popular in Magadha region and founded
by Makkhali Gosala.
II. 	 The followers of this sect held that the affairs
of the entire universe were ordered by a cosmic force called niyati.
III. 	Ajivikas practised austerities rather than
pursue any purposeful goal.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Ajivikas or ‘Followers of the way of life,’ were an ascetic order that started at the time of Buddha and Mahavira, and lasted until the 14th century. **Ajivika was founded by Makkhali Gosala.** Ajivikas were very **popular in Magadha in the third century BCE** and Mauryan kings donated several caves in the honour of Ajivika monks. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ According to tradition, it is believed that for some six years, Mahavira’s hardship was shared by Gosala, but ultimately the two quarreled and Gosala left Mahavira to found the sect of the Ajivikas (pre-determinism or ‘niyati’). His doctrines and those of his followers are known only from
Buddhist and Jaina sources, which state that he was lowborn and died after a quarrel with Mahavira shortly before the Buddha died.
● The Ajivikas, who **were rigid fatalists and determinists, supposedly held that the affairs of the entire** **universe were ordered by a cosmic force called niyati** (meaning ‘rule’ or ‘destiny’ in Sanskrit) that determined all events, including an individual’s fate, to the last detail and that barred personal efforts to change or accelerate improvement toward one’s spiritual destiny. No human effort could have any effect against niyati, and therefore karma is a fallacy. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Nirvana was only reached after living through an immense number of lives, which proceeded automatically like the unwinding of a ball of thread, the last life being as an Ajivika monk. As a result of this static and melancholy view of the human condition, the **Ajivikas practiced austerities rather than** **pursue any purposeful goal.** Some later Ajivikas worshipped Gosala as a divinity, and the tenet of niyati developed into the doctrine that all change was illusory and that everything was eternally immobile. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to the core philosophical concepts
of Buddhism, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Anicca refers to the impermanent nature of all
conditioned phenomena.
II. 	 Anatta denotes the absence of a permanent,
unchanging self or soul.
III. 	Dukkha signifies suffering or unsatisfactoriness inherent in worldly existence.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Buddhist philosophy is centred on understanding the nature of existence to attain liberation from suffering. The Buddha emphasised certain fundamental characteristics of reality that explain why human life is marked by dissatisfaction. These concepts are foundational to Buddhist thought and practice.
● **Anicca** means **impermanence and asserts that all conditioned things are transient.** Nothing in the world is permanent or eternal. Realising impermanence is essential for detachment and wisdom in Buddhism. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Anatta** refers to the doctrine of non-self, which **rejects the idea of an eternal soul.** According to Buddhism, what we consider the self is only a combination of changing physical and mental elements. This view distinguishes Buddhism from many other Indian philosophies. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Dukkha** represents **suffering, pain, or dissatisfaction inherent in worldly life.** Birth, ageing, disease, death, and unfulfilled desires are all forms of dukkha. Understanding dukkha is the first of the Four Noble Truths. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
“The world is made of soul (jiva) and matter
(ajiva), which are eternal. The coming together of jiva and ajiva create karma (action), which leads to an endless cycle of birth and rebirth. To free oneself from karma, one has to practice severe austerities and self-mortification”. The above concept of life is preached by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Jain philosophy is based on a **strict dualistic view of existence**, recognising jiva (living soul) and ajiva (non-living matter) as eternal entities.
● The interaction between jiva and ajiva results in the accumulation of karma, which binds the soul to the cycle of birth and rebirth. Liberation (moksha) is achieved through severe austerities, self-discipline, and ascetic practices that eliminate accumulated karma. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

● **Shishupalgarh**, however, is an ancient fortified settlement near Bhubaneswar and is not an edict site; nearby edicts in Odisha are found at Dhauli and Jaugada, not Shishupalgarh. T**herefore, option (d) is the answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to to the Sangam and post-
Sangam Tamil literature, consider the following
statements:
I. 	 Tolkappiyam is the earliest extant Tamil
grammatical work and deals with poetry as
well as the society and culture of its time.
II. 	 The Pathinen Kilkanakku belong to the
post-Sangam period and reflect a social and
cultural set-up different from the Sangam age.
Which of the above statements is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Tamil literature provides valuable insights into the linguistic, social, and cultural history of South India. The Sangam and post-Sangam periods represent two distinct phases, each marked by unique literary forms and societal structures.
● **Tolkappiyam is the earliest surviving Tamil grammatical text and is traditionally attributed to** **Tolkappiyar**. It deals not only with grammar and poetics but also with social practices, norms, and cultural values of the Sangam age. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Pathinen Kilkanakku texts and the five great Tamil epics belong to the post-Sangam period,** generally dated between the fourth and sixth centuries CE. These works portray changes in s**ocial** **organisation, religious ideas, and moral values** compared to earlier Sangam literature. **So, statement II** **is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to eclipses, consider the following
statements:
**Statement I:**
A total solar eclipse is the result of the apparent
angular size of the Moon.
**Statement II:**
The Moon orbits Earth in an elliptical path.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A total solar eclipse occurs when the Moon completely covers the Sun’s disc as seen from Earth.** This is possible only when the **Moon’s apparent angular diameter** (which ranges from ~29.3′ to 34.1′) becomes **larger than the Sun’s** (~31.6′ to 32.7′). **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Moon orbits Earth in an elliptical path,** causing its distance from Earth to vary between perigee (~363,300 km) and apogee (~405,500 km). This **variation changes the Moon’s apparent angular size**, enabling:
○ Total solar eclipses - when the Moon is near perigee — larger apparent size. ○ Annular eclipses - when Moon is near apogee — smaller apparent size.
**Knowledge Box**
**Types of Solar Eclipses**
Type Condition Appearance
Total Solar Eclipse
Moon’s apparent diameter > Sun’s Sun completely obscured; corona visible
Annular Solar Eclipse
Moon’s apparent diameter < Sun’s A bright ring (“annulus”) surrounds the Moon
Partial Solar Eclipse
Sun is partially covered Crescent-shaped Sun
Hybrid Eclipse Appears total at some locations & annular at others
Rare
**So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
With reference to volcanic eruptions, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 At convergent boundaries, subduction generates silica-rich viscous magma, leading to highly explosive volcanic eruptions.
II. 	 Shield volcanoes erupt low-viscosity basaltic
magma that spreads over vast areas.
III. 	Submarine volcanic eruptions do not contribute to the formation of new landmasses.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Volcanic activity is closely linked with plate tectonics, magma composition, and the physical properties of molten material. The explosivity and landform characteristics of eruptions depend on viscosity, gas content, and tectonic setting, all of which produce distinct volcanic features.
● **Convergent boundaries** involve subduction of oceanic crust, **producing magma enriched in silica and** **volatiles**. Such magma is highly viscous, traps gases, and **generates powerful explosive eruptions** typical of stratovolcanoes found in the Pacific Ring of Fire. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Shield volcanoes erupt low-viscosity basaltic magma that flows easily and covers vast areas**. This leads to broad, gently sloping landforms, as seen in the Hawaiian volcanic system and Icelandic basaltic fields. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Submarine volcanoes frequently create new landmasses.** Examples include Surtsey Island (Iceland, 1963), Nishinoshima (Japan), and emerging seamounts in the Pacific, all formed by underwater eruptions that gradually built up above sea level. **So, statement III is not correct.**
**Knowledge Box**
Types of Volcanoes: • **Shield Volcanoes**: Basaltic, fluid lava; broad slopes (Hawaii). • **Composite/Stratovolcanoes:** Silica-rich magma; explosive (Japan, Andes). • **Submarine Volcanoes**: Can form islands (Surtsey, Nishinoshima). • **Calderas**: Collapse structures after major eruptions (Crater Lake).
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Landscapes progress through all stages of youth, maturity, and old age under conditions of longterm tectonic stability.
**Statement II:** Continuous tectonic uplift accelerates the cycle of erosion and enables the landscape to reach the peneplain stage more quickly.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Landform evolution theories attempt to explain how landscapes transform over long time periods under the influence of erosion, tectonics, and climate. Davis’ model assumes a stable tectonic setting and uniform processes; any disturbance alters the expected sequence of landform stages.
● **The Davisian cycle assumes an uplifted landmass followed by prolonged tectonic stability during**
**which erosion gradually lowers relief.** This produces distinct youth, maturity, and old-age stages, ultimately leading to a peneplain if no renewed uplift occurs. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Continuous tectonic uplift disrupts rather than accelerates Davis’ cycle**. Ongoing uplift rejuvenates the landscape, creates new relief, and prevents the formation of a peneplain. Instead, such conditions align more closely with Penck’s or King’s models, where uplift and erosion operate simultaneously. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
**Knowledge Box**
Comparison of Landform Models: • Davis: Sequential stages → Assumes tectonic stability • Penck: Parallel retreat of slopes → Continuous uplift • King: Pediplanation → Sheet wash in arid climates
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
A particular City in India has the following
characteristics:
I. 	 It is located on the Tropic of Cancer.
II. 	 It was considered the "Greenwich of India"
by ancient Indian astronomers.
III. 	It is situated on the bank of the river that
travels from South to North.
Which one among the following cities has all the above characteristics?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ujjain** is situated on a unique geographical location from where the **tropic of cancer passes**. It is the **‘Greenwich Mean Time’ of India for Panchang**. The tilting of earth at angle of 23½ ◦on its axis and geographical line of tropic of cancer has special cosmic influence making it fit for absolute time location.
● Situated on the **bank of river Kshipra**, the river that travels straight from South to North, Ujjain reflects a distinct character of socio-religious culture.
● According to the ancient geographers of India, the Central Meridian, also known as the Prime Meridian, passes through Ujjain.
● Ujjain enjoyed a position of considerable importance in the field of astronomy, Great works on astronomy such as the **Surya Siddhanta and the Panch Siddhanta were written in Ujjain**.
● From about the 4th century B.C. **Ujjain enjoyed the reputation of being India’s Greenwich**. The **observatory was constructed by Maharaja Sawai Raja Jaisingh of Jaipur in 1719 when he was in** **Ujjain as the Governor of Malwa** under the reign of king Muhammad Shah of Delhi. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
Auroras occur predominantly in high-latitude
regions.
**Statement II:**
Earth’s magnetic field lines converge near the
poles.
**Statement III:**
High-energy charged particles from the
solar wind collide with atmospheric gases.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct, but only one of them explains
Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Auroras** represent interactions between Earth’s magnetosphere and incoming charged particles from the Sun, producing vivid light displays observable mainly near polar regions. Their distribution and intensity depend on geomagnetic conditions and solar activity.
● Auroras are most commonly observed near the **polar regions** (the auroral ovals around the Arctic and Antarctic) because these areas are where charged particles from space are most likely to enter Earth’s atmosphere. The structure of Earth’s magnetic field directs these particles toward high latitudes rather than equatorial regions. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Earth’s magnetic field resembles that of a bar magnet, with field lines **converging and becoming denser** **near the magnetic poles**. This convergence acts as a funnel, guiding charged particles from the magnetosphere toward the upper atmosphere in polar regions. **So, statement II is correct.**
● When charged particles from the **solar wind** are guided along magnetic field lines into Earth’s upper atmosphere, they **collide with gases such as oxygen and nitrogen**. These collisions excite the gas atoms
and molecules, which emit light when they return to their normal energy state, producing the visible auroral displays. **So, statement III is correct.**
● Auroras occur at high latitudes **because** Earth’s magnetic field lines converge near the poles, allowing solar wind particles to collide with atmospheric gases **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
With reference to Typhoons, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 It is a non-frontal spatially large low-pressure
system with organized convection.
II. 	 It typically forms where sea surface
temperatures exceed 26°C.
III. 	Ocean warming leads to frequent and rapid
intensification of typhoons.
IV. 	 All typhoons rotate clockwise in the Northern
Hemisphere due to the Coriolis effect.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Tropical cyclones require warm ocean waters, high humidity, and sufficient Coriolis force to develop, and their behaviour is strongly influenced by ocean-atmosphere interactions. Recent climate research highlights increasing cyclone intensity linked to warming seas.
● The terms "hurricane" and "typhoon" are regionally specific names for a strong "tropical cyclone". **A** **tropical cyclone is the generic term for a non-frontal synoptic scale low-pressure system over** **tropical or sub-tropical waters with organized convection** (i.e. thunderstorm activity) and definite cyclonic surface wind circulation. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The western North Pacific is the warmest and most extensive tropical ocean basin, providing ideal** **conditions, SST above 26°C,** high humidity, and sufficient Coriolis force for typhoon formation. This region produces nearly one-third of all global tropical cyclones annually. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Studies by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), and stands for the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), show that **warmer upper-ocean layers have** **increased the frequency of rapid intensification events.** Higher ocean heat content provides abundant latent heat, enabling cyclones to strengthen quickly before landfall, raising disaster risks. **So, statement** **III is correct.**
● **Typhoons rely on warm, moist ocean air for energy; once over land, they lose access to this heat** **source.** Increased friction and disrupted inflow/outflow patterns further weaken the system, causing a rapid drop in wind speed and organisation.
● **Typhoons,** like all Northern Hemisphere tropical cyclones, **rotate counterclockwise** due to the Coriolis effect. Clockwise rotation occurs only in the Southern Hemisphere. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD),
consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is characterized by sea surface temperature
differences between the Pacific Ocean and the Indian Ocean.
II. 	 A positive IOD event strengthens the
monsoon winds moving towards the Indian subcontinent.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD)** is a climate phenomenon characterized by **sea surface temperature** **differences** between the **western and eastern Indian Ocean**.  **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ A **positive IOD** occurs when the western Indian Ocean is warmer than usual and the eastern Indian Ocean is cooler, which can enhance monsoon rainfall in India. ○ Conversely, a **negative IOD** is when the western side is cooler and the eastern side is warmer, potentially leading to reduced rainfall in India. It interacts with global climate systems and significantly influences the Indian monsoon.
● **A positive IOD warms the western Indian Ocean** while cooling waters near Indonesia, strengthening the cross-equatorial flow and **enhancing monsoon winds towards India.** This often improves monsoon rainfall, even during an El Niño year, by increasing moisture availability over the Bay of Bengal and Arabian Sea. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Although the IOD is an independent phenomenon, it often interacts with the El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO). A strong positive IOD can partly counteract the adverse impacts of El Niño on the Indian monsoon, while **a negative IOD can worsen monsoon deficits during El Niño events,** showing that they are not truly independent.
● During a negative IOD, warmer waters and stronger convection develop near Indonesia and northern Australia, leading to enhanced rainfall in those regions. At the same time, the western Indian Ocean becomes cooler, reducing convection and causing below-normal rainfall over East Africa, particularly in countries like Kenya and Ethiopia. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Drainage System, Rivers, River Interlinking
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct
with regard to the Brahmani River?
I. 	 It is formed by the confluence of the Sankh
and South Koel rivers.
II. 	 The Brahmani delta is the site of the
Bhitarkanika Wildlife Sanctuary.
III. 	The principal tributaries of this river are
Sankh, Tikra, and Karo
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Brahmani River is formed by the confluence of the Sankh and South Koel rivers and flows** **through Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, and Odisha** before entering the Bay of Bengal as part of the Mahanadi–Baitarani–Brahmani delta system. Its tributary network consists of smaller rivers draining the Chota Nagpur Plateau and Eastern Ghats. **So, statement I is correct.**
● It is one of the major rivers in eastern India, primarily flowing through the state of Odisha. The river is known as Dhamra in its lower reaches.
● Both the sources of the Brahmani River are on the Chota Nagpur Plateau. The Sankh River has its origins near the Jharkhand-Chhattisgarh border, along with the South Koel River, which also rises in Jharkhand.
● The basin flows through Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, and Odisha states, draining a total area of 39,033 sq.km. before it outflows into the Bay of Bengal.
● Together with the rivers Mahanadi and Baitarani, it forms a large delta before emptying into the Bay of Bengal at Palmyras Point, Odisha.  **The Brahmani delta is the site of the Bhitarkanika Wildlife** **Sanctuary**, famous for its estuarine crocodiles. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Brahmani River basin is bounded in the north by the Chhotanagpur plateau, in the west and south by the Mahanadi basin, and in the east by the Bay of Bengal.
● **The principal tributaries of this river are Sankh, Tikra, and Karo**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Great Nicobar Island:
I. 	 It is the northernmost island of the Nicobar
Archipelago.
II. 	 The Shompen tribes live in the forests of the
Great Nicobar Biosphere Reserve.
III. 	Saddle Peak, the highest elevation of the
Andaman and Nicobar Islands, is located on this island.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Great Nicobar **is the southernmost island of the Nicobar Islands Archipelago.** It is home to a very rich ecosystem, including 650 species of angiosperms, ferns, gymnosperms, bryophytes, among others. In terms of fauna, there are over 1800 species, some of which are endemic to this area. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● **The Great Nicobar Biosphere Reserve** harbours a wide spectrum of ecosystems comprising tropical wet evergreen forests, mountain ranges reaching a **height of 642 m (Mt. Thullier) above sea level**, and coastal plains. It is home to the well-known Crab-eating Macaque, Nicobar Tree Shrew, Dugong, Nicobar
Megapode, Serpent Eagle, salt water crocodile, marine turtles and Reticulated Python are endemic and/or endangered.
○ **The highest elevation is 2,418 feet (737 metres) at Saddle Peak on North Andaman,** followed by Mount Thullier at 2,106 feet (642 metres) on Great Nicobar and Mount Harriet at 1,197 feet (365 metres) on South Andaman. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **The Mongoloid Shompen Tribe, about 200 in number, live in the forests of the biosphere reserve,** **particularly along the rivers and streams.** They are hunters and food gatherers, dependent on forest and marine resources for sustenance.
○ Another Mongoloid Tribe, Nicobarese, about 300 in number, used to live in settlements along the west coast. The Shompens move between the Core and Buffer Zones, while the settlers and Nicobarese live in settlements spread along the coast in the Transition zone. **So, statement II is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The Eastern Himalayan Syntaxis (EHS) marks a
zone where the northward-moving Indian Plate
continues to collide with and underthrust the
Eurasian Plate.
**Statement II:**
The convergence rate of continental plates is
faster in the Eastern Himalayas than the average
across the Himalayas.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Eastern Himalayan Syntaxis (EHS)**—located around the **Namcha Barwa–Tsangpo** **(Brahmaputra) bend**—is indeed a zone where the **northward-moving Indian Plate continues to** **collide with and underthrust the Eurasian Plate**. This is a classic case of **continental–continental** **convergence**, leading to intense crustal shortening, uplift, deep thrusting, rapid exhumation, and very high seismicity. The term syntaxis refers to the sharp bending and termination of Himalayan structural trends at this eastern end, not to any change from convergence to divergence. So, **Statement I is correct.**
● There is **no conclusive evidence** that the **plate convergence rate** between the Indian and Eurasian plates is **faster in the Eastern Himalayas** than elsewhere along the Himalayan arc. The average convergence rate (roughly **40–50 mm/year**) is broadly similar across the Himalayas.  So, **Statement II is not correct.**
● What distinguishes the EHS is **not a higher plate convergence rate**, but: ○ **Geometric focusing of strain** due to the sharp bend of the orogen, ○ Interaction with the **Indo–Burman ranges** and nearby tectonic blocks, ○ Extremely rapid **erosion and exhumation**, which amplify deformation signals.
● Thus, the exceptional tectonic activity at the EHS arises from **structural complexity and strain** **localization**, not from a locally faster convergence rate. **So, statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following ecological adaptations
seen in plants:
I. 	 Releasing volatile organic chemicals
II. 	 Leaves modified into spines
III. 	Pneumatophores in roots
IV. 	 Needle like leaves
V. 	 Use of camouflage method
How many of the above are used by plants to
protect themselves from herbivores?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Predation** is an ecological interaction where one organism, the predator, kills and eats another organism, the prey. Predators play a crucial role in maintaining the population of prey species, preventing them from overgrazing or destabilizing the ecosystem. Predation as nature’s way of transferring to higher trophic levels the energy fixed by plants.
○ **For plants, herbivores are the predators**. **Nearly 25 percent of all insects are known to be** **phytophagous** (feeding on plant sap and other parts of plants). The problem is particularly severe
for plants because, unlike animals, they cannot run away from their predators. Plants therefore have evolved an astonishing variety of morphological and chemical defences against herbivores.
● When attacked, many plants (For ex, maize, acacia) release **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs).** These act as a "distress call" to attract predators (like wasps) that eat the herbivores, or to warn neighboring plants to produce toxic tannins.
○ Many plants produce and store chemicals that make the herbivore sick when they are eaten, inhibit feeding or digestion, disrupt its reproduction or even kill it. ○ **For example**, Milkweeds (**Calotropis**) produce highly poisonous cardiac glycosides and thus, any cattle or goats do not browse on this plant. **Nicotine, caffeine, quinine, strychnine and opium,** **are produced by plants as defense against grazers and browsers. So, point I is correct.**
● **Morphological adaptations like spines and thorns** (modified leaves, For Ex, in Cactus) create a sharp physical barrier that makes it painful and difficult for animals to graze on the plant. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Pneumatophores** are **respiratory roots** (breathing roots) found in mangrove plants (Halophytes) like **Rhizophora**. They grow vertically upwards from the mud to help the plant get oxygen for respiration, as the saline, marshy soil is oxygen deficient. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Needle-like leaves** are primarily an adaptation to reduce water loss (transpiration), (For Ex, in Pines). These leaves are tough, have a small surface area, and are often resinous/waxy. This **makes them** **unpalatable and difficult for generalist herbivores** to chew and digest. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Some plants use crypsis (i.e., camouflage)** to avoid herbivores. For example, the sensitive plant (**Mimosa** **pudica**) mimics the appearance of a dead or wilting plant by folding and drooping its leaves when touched. Similarly, **stone plants** (**Lithops**) decrease appearance by resembling stones. **So, point V is correct.** **So, only four of the above ecological adaptations are used by plants as defence against herbivory.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Insectivorous Plants**
● The term “insectivorous” was used by Charles Darwin to characterize a group of plants that seemed to trap and feed on insects.
● The carnivorous habit in plants is not obligate, and **carnivorous plants can grow without an insect** **meal,** depending instead on photosynthesis and minerals supplied from the soil.
● In general, carnivorous plants grow in mineral-deficient, sometimes sandy soils. ○ The carnivorous plant habitat is typically low in nitrogen, phosphorus and in potassium. ○ In this sort of habitat, plants that have alternative strategies for obtaining essential minerals are at a competitive advantage.
○ In most carnivorous plants, the trap represents a modification of the entire leaf or of structures borne on the leaf.
● There are approximately 700 identified species of carnivorous plants. In India, a total of 44 species of carnivorous plants are reported.
● The three major **Insectivorous Plants** families represented in India are **Nepenthaceae**, **Droseraceae**, and **Lentibulariaceae**.
○ **Pitcher Plants** (Nepenthaceae) is the only native pitcher plant species that exists in India.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Protected Area State
I. - Dampa Tiger Reserve Manipur
II. - Papikonda National Park Andhra Pradesh
III. - Sri Venkateswara Wildlife Sanctuary
Karnataka
IV. - Raimona National Park Assam
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Dampa Tiger Reserve is in Mizoram.** The moist valleys harbour **tropical evergreen forests** while the steeper slopes have more **deciduous** elements, often with sympodial bamboos in the under storey. The habitat is drained by the **river** Khawthlangtuipui in the West and the Teirei River to the East.
○ The major fauna includes Hoolock Gibbon, Rhesus Macaque, Assamese Macaque, Pig-Tailed Macaque and Chinese Pangolin. Dampa is connected with the **Thorang Wildlife Sanctuary** in the Southeast. After the 2018 tiger census there is no proof of the tiger at the reserve. **So, pair I is** **not correctly matched.**
● **Papikonda National Park** is in the East and West Godavari districts of **Andhra Pradesh**. It lies on either side of the **river Godavari** in Eastern Ghats encompassing the famous **Papi hill range**. The climatic condition is tropical wet and dry climate. The vegetation is predominantly Moist Deciduous.
○ The **Polavaram Irrigation Project** is currently submerging a significant portion of this park. It is a recognized habitat for **migratory birds,** including **river terns** and **painted storks**. **Indian** **Leopard**, **Indian Giant Squirrel and Sloth Bear** are prominent animals found here. **So, pair II** **is correctly matched.**
● **Sri Venkateswara National Park** is a part of **Seshachalam Hills Biosphere Reserve.** The National Park is in **Eastern Ghats** spread over the Seshachalam hills of Cuddapah district and Tirumala hills of Chittoor district of **Andhra Pradesh**. **Red Sanders and the Slender Loris** are prominent flora and fauna found here.
○ It is known for its waterfalls like Talakona, Gundalakona, and Gunjana. It falls within the catchment of the **Penna River basin**. Through **Rayala Elephant Reserve corridor,** it is connected with **Koundinya Wildlife Sanctuary. So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Raimona in Kokrajhar district has become Assam’s sixth national park and 106th national park in** **India.** **Raimona National Park** is located at the southern foothills of Eastern Himalaya and forms a boundary with Phibsoo Wildlife Sanctuary of Bhutan and Buxa Tiger Reserve of West Bengal. It is bounded by the **Sankosh River on the west** (bordering West Bengal) and the **Saralbhanga River on the** **east.** Both are tributaries of the Brahmaputra. The **Pekua river** defines Raimona’s southern boundary.
○ It is famous for the **Golden Langur** (Endemic to Assam). Raimona is an integral part of the Manas Biosphere Reserve and the Chirang-Ripu Elephant Reserve. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**107th National Park of India**
● **Similipal Tiger Reserve** (STR) is the 107th national park and the second in Odisha, after Bhitarkanika.
● It gives shelter to 25% of Odisha’s elephant population and 104 orchid species, many endemics to the region.
● The forests of Similipal are a mix of sal trees, moist deciduous, and semi-evergreen types.
● It is the world’s only home to wild melanistic (black) tigers.
**Relevance:** Top Maoist leader Madvi Hidma, wife among 6 killed in encounter at Papikonda National Park in Andhra Pradesh.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Mass Extinctions and IUCN Red List
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard
to the Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) Alliance:
I. 	 It is a global partnership consisting of BirdLife
International, Conservation International, IUCN, and UNEP-WCMC.
II. 	 It provides trusted data to assess biodiversity
risks and align with global frameworks to support nature-positive goals.
III. 	It works under the secretariat of the United
Nations Environment Programme.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT)** is a **multi-institutional alliance** formed by: Birdlife International, Conservation International, the International Union of Conservation for Nature (IUCN), and the United Nations Environment Programme World Conservation Monitoring Centre (UNEP-WCMC). It is headquartered in the David Attenborough Building (DAB) in Cambridge, UK. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **IBAT** is designed as **a decision-support tool.** IBAT is designed to facilitate access to critical biodiversity data for the private sector and finance institutions. It helps them perform biodiversity risk assessments and align their projects with international standards like the **International Finance Corporation** **(IFC)**Performance Standard 6 and the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF). It is a key tool for corporations aiming for "Nature Positive" outcomes. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ It acts as a "one-stop shop" or single access point for three critical global datasets: ○ IUCN Red List of Threatened Species ○ World Database on Protected Areas (WDPA) ○ World Database of Key Biodiversity Areas
● **IBAT does not work under the specific secretariat of the United Nations Environment Programme** **(UNEP).** It is a multi-institutional partnership and functions as an independent collaboration funded by subscription fees from users (corporations/banks) to maintain the database. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The IBAT Alliance, announced on June 23, 2025, that its 2024 investment in biodiversity data reached a record level of $2.5 million — an increase from $1.2 million in 2023.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Wildlife Conservation (Tiger, Elephant, Lion, Rhino, Crocodile, Vulture, Tortoise, Dolphin, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Regarding Gharial (Gavialis gangeticus), consider
the following statements:
I. 	 They are not covered under the Integrated
Development of Wildlife Habitats Scheme for Protection of Wildlife.
II. 	 It is the only known crocodilian species that
shares its habitat with the Saltwater Crocodile in the Sundarbans.
III. 	It is the only crocodilian which shows a
visible sexual dimorphism.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Gharial (IUCN status - Critically Endangered) is one of the priority species identified for** **recovery under the Integrated Development of Wildlife Habitats (IDWH) scheme.** IDWH is a **centrally sponsored scheme** that provides financial assistance for protecting wildlife and its habitats. Key components include supporting Protected Areas like national parks and sanctuaries, protecting wildlife outside these areas, and implementing **recovery programs for critically endangered species**.
○ **There are currently 22 critically endangered species in the country which are covered under** **this component.** These are Snow Leopard, Bustard (including Floricans), Dolphin, Hangul, Nilgiri Tahr, Marine Turtles, Dugongs, Edible Nest Swiftlet, Asian Wild Buffalo, Nicobar Megapode, Manipur Brow-antlered Deer, Vultures, Malabar Civet, Indian Rhinoceros, Asiatic Lion, Swamp Deer, Jerdon’s Courser, Northern River Terrapin, Clouded Leopard, Arabian Sea Humpback Whale, Red Panda and Caracal. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Gharials are exclusively freshwater animals**. They inhabit deep, fast-flowing rivers. Saltwater Crocodiles (**Crocodylus porosus**) inhabit saline and brackish waters (estuaries, Sundarbans and Bhitarkanika). Gharials are important for a river’s ecosystem, as they clean up carrion. Between 1975 and 1982, India established 16 captive breeding and release centers and five gharial sanctuaries. Today, the species survives primarily in five refuges: National Chambal Sanctuary, Katerniaghat Sanctuary, Chitwan National Park, Son River Sanctuary, and Satkosia Gorge Sanctuary.
○ Nearly 435 km area of Chambal River has been declared **Chambal Gharial Sanctuary.** Chambal river flows on the border of Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, and Uttar Pradesh. At Deori Gharial Rearing Centre (Madhya Pradesh) eggs are hatched and reared. When the babies are 3 years old, they are released into the river.
○ About 80 percent of the total population of Skimmers (Bird) in the country migrate to the Chambal Sanctuary. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The **Gharial is the only crocodilian species that shows visible sexual dimorphism** (distinct physical difference between male and female). Adult **female gharials are 2.6 to 4.5 metres** long and **male gharials** **are 3 to 6 metres long.** Adult males have a distinct tip at the end of their snout, which resembles an earthen pot called a ghada (pot), hence the name gharial. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India marks 50 years of its Crocodile Conservation Programme in 2025.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to the classification of industries
by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB),
consider the following statements:
I. 	 Industries are classified by CPCB based
on Pollution Index, a matrix which was
introduced in 2016.
II. 	 Common Effluent Treatment Plants and
Sewage Treatment Plants are classified under
the 'Green' category.
III. 	Blue Category industries are involved in
management of environmental pollution
arising from domestic activities.
IV. 	 Industries classified under the 'White'
category are exempted from the requirement
of obtaining both 'Consent to Establish' and
'Consent to Operate'.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** is a statutory body constituted in September 1974 under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.** The CPCB introduced the **Pollution Index** **(PI) matrix** in 2016 to classify industries. The PI is a number from 0 to 100 derived from the weightage of air pollutants, water pollutants, and hazardous waste.
○ The category of the sector is decided based on the pollution index range, if PI ≥ 80 the category of sector is Red, if PI ranges between 55 ≤ PI < 80, the category of sector is orange, similarly for the range of PI between 25 ≤ PI < 55, the category is Green and for PI < 25, the category of the sector is white. ○ **In the revised classification (2025),** CPCB has classified a total of 419 sectors into Red (125), Orange (137), Green (94), White (54) and Blue (9) categories. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Green category industries** are those that are considered less polluting, with a Pollution Index score between 25 and 55. They are pollution abatement facilities, they collect and treat massive loads of hazardous and toxic waste. If they malfunction, they become significant point-sources of pollution, requiring the strictest regulatory surveillance.
○ However, Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETPs) and Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) are classified under the Blue Category of industries, which represents essential environmental services for the abatement of pollution. **So, statement II is not correct.**
